acceptance_targets: [CH2-F01] Pass if revised chapter_02 no longer presents Park Ji-eun as physically present and speaking in the studio sequence (lines 229-341 equivalent), and liability prompts no longer target PARK JIEUN by name (line 359 equivalent). Fail if Ji-eun remains an active scene participant in chapter_02 timeline.

[CH2-F02] Pass if the revised 399-405 equivalent includes at least two concrete consequences (for example: explicit recovery trigger, named account hold, direct human reaction) and avoids abstract moral-summary phrasing as the primary beat. Fail if the ending remains mostly conceptual commentary on harm.

[CH2-F03] Pass if revised lines 369-408 equivalent contain a direct Ina-family interaction that names the billing intervention and forces an immediate decision/rupture before the chapter ends. Fail if the chapter still jumps from alerts to travel summary without that confrontation.

[CH2-F04] Pass if revised passages identify who legally authorizes each transfer step, what contractual clause/workflow enables rapid execution, and one explicit friction point (review, cap, or rollback risk). Fail if consequences still appear instantaneous with no legible operational path.

[CH2-F05] Pass if revised versions of the cited beats use contractions as baseline (for example: don't, I'll, I'm) and any retained full-form phrasing is clearly motivated by deliberate emphasis. Fail if these confrontation lines remain predominantly formal full forms.

[CH2-F06] Pass if, when reading the revised cluster without speaker tags, each line remains attributable to a single character by diction and rhythm alone. Fail if at least two lines could be swapped between speakers with little change in plausibility.

[CH2-F07] Pass if no more than two laugh-as-reaction constructions remain across the cited lines and replacements reveal distinct character pressure (anger, contempt, disbelief) through non-redundant behavior. Fail if three or more near-identical laugh beats persist.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if revised chapter_02 no longer presents Park Ji-eun as physically present and speaking in the studio sequence (lines 229-341 equivalent), and liability prompts no longer target PARK JIEUN by name (line 359 equivalent). Fail if Ji-eun remains an active scene participant in chapter_02 timeline.
findings.problem: Continuity contradiction: Park Ji-eun is onstage as an active pilot-table performer in chapter 2, but canon continuity places her first on-page availability from chapter 4 onward. This breaks timeline trust and destabilizes downstream state transitions tied to her debut and coercion arc.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the studio scene spans at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:229-341 and notification lines at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:359-361. Option A: replace Park Ji-eun with an unnamed/other performer in this chapter and remove performer-specific debt transfer stakes. Option B: if Ji-eun must appear, reframe as offstage reference only (no physical presence, no direct dialogue) and defer first embodied encounter to chapter 4-consistent placement.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised 399-405 equivalent includes at least two concrete consequences (for example: explicit recovery trigger, named account hold, direct human reaction) and avoids abstract moral-summary phrasing as the primary beat. Fail if the ending remains mostly conceptual commentary on harm.
findings.problem: Sanitization drift at the chapter close: the prose retreats into abstract ethical summary at the exact moment where the narrative has set up concrete coercive consequence. This softens impact through conceptual language instead of embodied fallout.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite the ending block at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:399-405 to stage immediate material consequence in-scene: a concrete financial/legal/mechanical effect landing on a specific body or relationship right after CONFIRM ASSUMPTION. Keep moral ambiguity, but remove exculpatory abstraction and replace with observable cause-effect detail.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if revised lines 369-408 equivalent contain a direct Ina-family interaction that names the billing intervention and forces an immediate decision/rupture before the chapter ends. Fail if the chapter still jumps from alerts to travel summary without that confrontation.
findings.problem: Timidity/cutaway at peak relational pressure: the chapter tees up a volatile family confrontation (hospital call to father, fraud panic) and then defers the encounter off-page, reducing immediate narrative pressure.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the end sequence at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:369-408 to include at least one on-page exchange (call or in-person) where Ina must answer for the guarantor intervention before scene exit. Keep chapter boundary to chapter 3, but make chapter 2 pay one relational cost in real time.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if revised passages identify who legally authorizes each transfer step, what contractual clause/workflow enables rapid execution, and one explicit friction point (review, cap, or rollback risk). Fail if consequences still appear instantaneous with no legible operational path.
findings.problem: Causal compression undermines plausibility: liability transfer, hospital posting, guarantor registration, and recovery-action warning all execute within minutes of a first-night probation signing with little procedural friction, reading as convenience rather than system behavior.
findings.rewrite_direction: Strengthen mechanism clarity in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:317-326 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:353-394 by naming the enabling instrument(s), the authorizing actor(s), and at least one realistic gate/checkpoint. Preserve urgency, but show why speed is possible in this world instead of assuming it.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if revised versions of the cited beats use contractions as baseline (for example: don't, I'll, I'm) and any retained full-form phrasing is clearly motivated by deliberate emphasis. Fail if these confrontation lines remain predominantly formal full forms.
findings.problem: Contraction/register drift for Ina: a high-contraction character under stress repeatedly lands on stiff full-form phrasing in hot exchanges, flattening spoken realism against the style-bible default contraction use.
findings.rewrite_direction: Adjust Ina's confrontation lines at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:62 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:146 to default spoken contractions unless a line is explicitly legalistic/emphatic. Keep force, but let pressure break syntax in her established idiolect.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, when reading the revised cluster without speaker tags, each line remains attributable to a single character by diction and rhythm alone. Fail if at least two lines could be swapped between speakers with little change in plausibility.
findings.problem: Idiolect separation softens in key exchanges: several voices converge into polished aphoristic one-liners, making speakers briefly interchangeable instead of distinctly register-bound.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the exchange cluster at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:335-340 (and nearby Cho/manager beats at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:321-326) so each speaker carries distinct lexical terrain: Ji-eun via performance/body language framing, Ina via craft/editorial framing, manager via contractual-risk diction, Cho via room-floor production pragmatics.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if no more than two laugh-as-reaction constructions remain across the cited lines and replacements reveal distinct character pressure (anger, contempt, disbelief) through non-redundant behavior. Fail if three or more near-identical laugh beats persist.
findings.problem: Recurring prose tic: the chapter reuses the same laugh-beat construction (short/one plus laugh) multiple times, which flattens reaction texture and signals formulaic cadence.
findings.rewrite_direction: Vary reaction mechanics at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:86, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:192, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:311 (optionally adjust snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_02.md:164). Replace at least two laugh beats with differentiated physical or vocal responses tied to motive.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: Lean into uncomfortable specificity when stakes demand it: show coercion mechanics, sexual negotiation, grief rituals, and financial desperation at scene level. In edge cases, prefer precision over decorum; protect character dignity through perspective accuracy, not by omitting the hard material.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: If a scene requires bodily, economic, or moral consequence and the draft retreats into abstract phrasing, rewrite with concrete action, sensation, and decision impact before proceeding.
aesthetic_risk_policy.operational_resolution_rule: When uncertain between tasteful distance and narrative truth, choose the version that preserves causal accountability and embodied consequence, then tune language for clarity rather than softness.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: high - contractions are the baseline in spoken dialogue; uncontracted forms are reserved for deliberate emphasis, legal language, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate chapter structures deliberately: high-density dialogue vs interior reflection, multi-scene public collision vs two-scene intimate pressure, aftermath inventory vs anticipatory dread. Never let two consecutive chapters share the same pacing architecture or turn mechanism.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, unsentimental literary diction that permits technical vocabulary, profanity, and class-marked speech without flattening idiolects.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: close third person centered on Seo Ina, with brief controlled widenings in public-event chapters.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Alternating pressure waves: compressed clauses in operational stress, longer breath-based lines in grief and reflection.
